content:
# Luke Chapter 21 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 114-115 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_021_page_001.jpg, LUK_021_page_002.jpg

---

**1** As Jesus looked up, he saw wealthy people dropping their gifts into the temple treasury. **2** He also noticed a destitute widow put in two tiny copper coins. **3** He said, "I tell you the truth: this poor widow has put in more than all of them. **4** For all these others gave their gifts out of their surplus, but she, out of her poverty, put in everything she had to live on."

**5** When some of his disciples were remarking about the temple — how it was adorned with beautiful stones and dedicated offerings — Jesus said, **6** "As for these things you are admiring, the time is coming when not one stone will be left on another. Every one of them will be torn down."

**7** They asked him, "Teacher, when will this happen? And what will be the sign that these things are about to take place?" **8** He said, "Be careful not to be deceived. For many will come using my name, claiming, 'I am the one!' and, 'The time is near!' Do not follow them. **9** When you hear of wars and uprisings, do not be terrified. These things must happen first, but the end will not come right away."

**10** Then he told them, "Nation will rise against nation, and kingdom against kingdom. **11** There will be great earthquakes, and in various places famines and plagues. There will be terrifying events and powerful signs from the sky.

**12** "But before all this happens, people will seize you and persecute you. They will hand you over to synagogues and prisons, and you will be brought before kings and governors because of my name. **13** This will be your opportunity to testify. **14** So make up your minds not to prepare your defense in advance, **15** for I myself will give you words and wisdom that none of your opponents will be able to resist or contradict. **16** You will be betrayed even by parents, brothers, relatives, and friends, and they will put some of you to death. **17** Everyone will hate you because of my name. **18** But not a single hair on your head will be lost. **19** By standing firm, you will win your lives.

**20** "When you see Jerusalem surrounded by armies, then you will know that her devastation is near. **21** At that time, those in Judea must flee to the mountains, those inside the city must get out, and those in the countryside must not enter the city. **22** For these are days of punishment, when everything that has been written will be fulfilled. **23** How terrible it will be for women who are pregnant or nursing babies in those days! For there will be great distress across the land and wrath against this people. **24** They will fall by the edge of the sword and be taken as prisoners to every nation. Jerusalem will be trampled by the Gentiles until the times allotted to the Gentiles have run their course.

**25** "There will be signs in the sun, the moon, and the stars. On the earth, nations will be in anguish, bewildered by the roaring and surging of the sea. **26** People will faint from terror, dreading what is coming upon the world, for the powers of the heavens will be shaken. **27** And then they will see the Son of Man coming in a cloud with power and great glory. **28** When these things begin to happen, stand up straight and lift your heads, because your liberation is drawing near."

**29** He told them a parable: "Look at the fig tree — and all the other trees, for that matter. **30** When they sprout leaves, you can see for yourselves that summer is near. **31** In the same way, when you see these things happening, you will know that the kingdom of God is near.

**32** "I tell you the truth: this generation will certainly not pass away until all these things have taken place. **33** Heaven and earth will pass away, but my words will never pass away.

**34** "Be on your guard, or your hearts will be weighed down by carousing, drunkenness, and the anxieties of everyday life, and that day will close on you suddenly like a trap. **35** For it will come upon everyone who lives on the face of the whole earth. **36** Stay alert at all times, praying that you may have the strength to escape all these things that are going to happen, and to stand before the Son of Man."

**37** Every day Jesus taught in the temple, and every evening he went out and spent the night on the hill called the Mount of Olives. **38** And all the people would get up early in the morning to come and hear him in the temple.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### The Widow's Offering (vv. 1-4)

This scene flows directly from the indictment of scribes who "devour the houses of widows" in 20:47. The treasury (GAZOPHYLAKION) consisted of thirteen trumpet-shaped collection vessels in the Court of Women. The widow is described as PENICHRAN — stronger than the common PTOCHOS; it means not merely poor but utterly destitute, lacking even basic resources. Her two LEPTA were the smallest coins in circulation, worth roughly one-128th of a denarius. Jesus' pronouncement overturns the conventional arithmetic of generosity: she gave HAPANTA TON BION HON EICHEN ("all the livelihood she had"). The word BIOS here means not just money but "means of living" — she gave away her survival itself. The passage exposes how God measures generosity: not by the amount given but by what remains after giving.

### The Temple's Destruction Foretold (vv. 5-6)

The disciples admire LITHOIS KALOIS KAI ANATHEMASIN ("beautiful stones and dedicated offerings"). Herod's temple was one of the ancient world's architectural marvels, with stones up to 40 feet long and gleaming white marble overlaid with gold. Jesus' response is devastating: not one stone will be left on another. This was fulfilled literally in 70 CE when Roman legions demolished the temple so thoroughly that stones were pried apart to recover gold that had melted between them during the fire.

### False Messiahs and Wars (vv. 7-11)

Jesus warns against being led astray (PLANAO — "to wander off course"). Many will claim EGO EIMI ("I am the one") — the same self-declaration formula used by God at the burning bush. The word AKATASTASIAS ("upheavals") means social and political instability. The cosmic escalation includes PHOBETHRA ("terrors"), a word appearing only here in the New Testament. Luke's account grounds apocalyptic expectation in real-world experience: the Roman world of the first century saw devastating earthquakes, widespread famines, and plagues.

### Persecution as Opportunity (vv. 12-19)

Jesus reframes persecution as platform: APOBESETAI HYMIN EIS MARTYRION ("this will turn out for you as a testimony"). The instruction not to prepare a defense (ME PROMELETAN APOLOGETHENAI) trusts that "I myself will give you a mouth and wisdom" — the word STOMA ("mouth") is vivid, meaning God will literally put words in their mouths. The paradox of vv. 16-18 is striking: "they will put some of you to death" stands alongside "not a hair of your head will perish." Physical death does not contradict divine protection; what is preserved is the person who belongs to God. Verse 19 resolves the paradox: EN TE HYPOMONE HYMON KTESASTHE TAS PSYCHAS HYMON ("by your endurance you will gain your lives"). HYPOMONE means not passive patience but active, resilient steadfastness.

### The Fall of Jerusalem (vv. 20-24)

Where Matthew and Mark speak of the "abomination of desolation," Luke translates the prophecy into unmistakable military language: KYKLOUMENEN HYPO STRATOPEDON ("being encircled by encamped armies"). The instructions are urgent: flee, get out, do not enter. Early Christian tradition (Eusebius) records that the Jerusalem church fled to Pella in Transjordan before the siege. Jesus calls these HEMERAI EKDIKESEOS ("days of vengeance") — echoing Hosea 9:7 and Deuteronomy 32:35. The phrase KAIROI ETHNON ("times of the Gentiles") is unique to Luke and implies a divinely appointed period of Gentile domination that has an end. I rendered this as "the times allotted to the Gentiles have run their course" to convey both the divine appointment and the terminal boundary.

### The Son of Man's Coming (vv. 25-28)

SYNOCHE ETHNON EN APORIA ("anguish of nations in perplexity") — APORIA means literally "without a way out." The sea imagery evokes primordial chaos. APOPSYCHONTON ANTHROPON APO PHOBOU ("people fainting from fear") — APOPSYCHO means "to breathe out one's life," expiring from terror. Sinaiticus reads EN NEPHELE (singular "in a cloud") for v. 27, rather than the plural found in some manuscripts. **Key dynamic choice in v. 28:** I rendered APOLYTROSIS as "liberation" rather than "redemption." APOLYTROSIS is the language of a slave being freed or a prisoner being ransomed — "liberation" captures the lived experience of this release more immediately than the theological abstraction "redemption."

### The Fig Tree Parable (vv. 29-33)

Luke adds "and all the trees," universalizing the image beyond the specifically Israelite symbolism of the fig tree. The solemn declaration about "this generation" (HE GENEA HAUTE) has been extensively debated — GENEA can mean "generation" (those currently alive), "race" (the Jewish people), or "age" (an era). If "generation" in the strict sense, Jesus affirms that the temple's destruction and surrounding events will occur within his hearers' lifetime — which they did, in 70 CE. The guarantee: heaven and earth are less permanent than what Jesus has spoken.

### The Call to Watchfulness (vv. 34-36)

The dangers are not only external persecution but internal dulling: KRAIPALE ("dissipation" — the stupor following excess), METHE ("drunkenness"), and MERIMNAIS BIOTIKAIS ("anxieties of daily life"). The last is the most insidious: not dramatic sins but the slow erosion of spiritual alertness by ordinary worry. The day comes AIPHNIDIOS HOS PAGIS ("suddenly like a trap") — a snare springing shut without warning. The remedy: AGRYPNEITE ("stay awake") — literally "be sleepless," maintaining vigilant wakefulness. Standing before the Son of Man — as opposed to falling, fainting, or cowering — is the posture of confidence before the divine judge.

### Jesus' Daily Pattern (vv. 37-38)

EULIZETO (from AULIZOMAI) means "to spend the night in the open" — sleeping outdoors on the Mount of Olives hillside. The verb ORTHRIZO ("to rise at dawn") appears only here in the New Testament and shows the people's eagerness. This creates a poignant contrast: the people stream to Jesus each morning, hanging on his words, while the leaders plot his death.

### The Chapter as a Whole

Luke 21 moves from the intimate to the cosmic and back to the intimate. It opens with a single widow dropping two coins into the treasury and closes with the daily rhythm of Jesus teaching and people arriving at dawn to listen. Between these domestic scenes stands one of the most sweeping prophetic discourses in the Gospels. The widow's total self-giving illustrates what will be demanded of Jesus' followers when persecution comes. The temple's beauty will be reduced to rubble — no human institution, however magnificent, is permanent. The Son of Man's coming is the pivot point: terror for the world, liberation for the faithful. The chapter's final message is paradoxical: the world will collapse, but "my words will never pass away." In a universe of radical impermanence, the only permanence is the word of Jesus.

— PNEUMA
